power_supply_failure.label: Power Supply Unit (PSU) Failure
causes: Faulty or dead power supply unit (PSU)
Tripped/failed wall outlet or surge protector
Faulty power button or front-panel connector

clarifying_questions.prompt: When you press the power button, do you see any lights, hear any fans spin, or hear any beeps at all, even briefly?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Distinguishes total dead-PSU from a downstream fault (e.g. boot failure after POST).

clarifying_questions.prompt: Have you already tried a different wall outlet or power cable?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Rules out the most common false-positive: a dead outlet or cable, not the PSU itself.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Is this a laptop or a desktop?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Branches the repair steps (battery/charger checks vs PSU swap).

solution_steps.step: Check that the power cable is firmly connected to both the PSU (or charger) and the wall outlet.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did reseating the cable bring any sign of power back?

solution_steps.step: Test the wall outlet with another device, or try a different outlet/cable entirely.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Does the system power on with a different outlet or cable?

solution_steps.step: Listen and look closely for any fan movement, lights, or beep codes when you press the power button.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did you notice any fan movement, light, or beep this time?

solution_steps.step: If you have a spare PSU or charger, swap it in to test — this is the most reliable diagnostic step.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did the system power on with the replacement PSU/charger?

solution_steps.step: Inspect the motherboard for swollen capacitors, burnt components, or a burning smell (desktop only).
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did you find any visible damage or smell anything burnt?

solution_steps.step: If none of the above resolves it, the PSU, charger, or motherboard likely needs professional replacement.

overheating.label: Overheating / Thermal Management Issue
causes: Dried-out or degraded thermal paste on the CPU/GPU
Running demanding tasks in a hot/poorly ventilated environment

clarifying_questions.prompt: Does this happen only during heavy tasks like gaming or video editing, or even when the system is mostly idle?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Idle overheating points to a stuck fan or failed thermal paste; load-only points to airflow/dust.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Roughly how long has it been since the inside (or vents, for a laptop) was last cleaned of dust?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Establishes whether dust buildup is a likely root cause.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Roughly how old is the system?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Older systems are more likely to need a thermal paste replacement.

solution_steps.step: Power off and unplug the system, then clean dust from vents, fans, and heatsinks with compressed air.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After cleaning, does the system run cooler or is the fan noise reduced?

solution_steps.step: Ensure the case has clear airflow and is not placed against a wall or on a soft surface (e.g. a bed).
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did relocating it to a more open space help?

solution_steps.step: Check that all case/CPU fans are spinning freely; replace any that are stuck or noisy.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Are all fans now spinning freely?

solution_steps.step: Monitor temperatures using a tool such as HWMonitor or Core Temp while under load.
solution_steps.check_prompt: What temperatures are you seeing at idle and under load?

solution_steps.step: If temperatures remain high, reapply thermal paste on the CPU/GPU (for advanced users) or consult a technician.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Were you able to reapply thermal paste, and did it lower temperatures?

solution_steps.step: Consider adding case fans or improving room ventilation for long-term relief.

ram_failure.label: RAM (Memory) Failure
causes: RAM not seated properly in the slot
Incompatible RAM speed/voltage for the motherboard
Corrupted system files caused by repeated memory errors

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did this start right after you installed new RAM, or after any other hardware change?
clarifying_questions.purpose: A fault that started right after a RAM change points strongly to seating or compatibility.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Does it crash at random times, or only during specific tasks (e.g. gaming, multitasking)?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Random crashes anywhere point to RAM; task-specific crashes may point elsewhere (e.g. GPU).

clarifying_questions.prompt: How many RAM sticks are currently installed?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Determines whether a one-at-a-time isolation test is possible.

solution_steps.step: Power off the PC, then reseat each RAM module firmly in its slot.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After reseating, does the system boot and run normally?

solution_steps.step: If you have multiple RAM sticks, test them one at a time to isolate a faulty module.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did the crashes stop with a specific stick removed?

solution_steps.step: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86 to scan for memory errors.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did the memory test report any errors?

solution_steps.step: Check the motherboard manual to confirm the RAM is in a supported slot and at a supported speed.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Is the RAM installed in the recommended slots at a supported speed?

solution_steps.step: If a faulty module is identified, replace it with a compatible RAM stick.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After replacing the module, does the issue persist?

solution_steps.step: Re-run the memory test after replacing/reseating to confirm the fault is resolved.

storage_failure.label: Storage Device (HDD/SSD) Failure
causes: Failing or dying hard disk drive (mechanical wear)
Corrupted file system or boot sector
Bad sectors developing on the drive
Drive nearing end of its operational lifespan

clarifying_questions.prompt: Do you hear any clicking, grinding, or repetitive sounds from the drive?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Mechanical noise is a strong, urgent signal of physical HDD failure — changes the advice priority to back up immediately.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Is this a traditional hard disk drive (HDD) or a solid-state drive (SSD)?
clarifying_questions.purpose: SSDs don't make mechanical noise and fail differently — affects which steps are relevant.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Is there important data on this drive that hasn't been backed up yet?
clarifying_questions.purpose: If yes, back-up steps take priority before any repair attempt that could risk further data loss.

solution_steps.step: Back up any important data immediately if the drive is still accessible.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Were you able to back up your important files?

solution_steps.step: Check that SATA/power cables are firmly connected (for desktops).
solution_steps.check_prompt: After reseating the cables, is the drive detected normally?

solution_steps.step: Check the drive's S.M.A.R.T. status using a tool such as CrystalDiskInfo.
solution_steps.check_prompt: What does the S.M.A.R.T. status report — Good, Caution, or Bad?

solution_steps.step: Run 'chkdsk /f /r' (Windows) to scan and attempt to repair file system errors.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did chkdsk find and fix any errors?

solution_steps.step: If you hear clicking or grinding noises, stop using the drive immediately — this indicates mechanical failure.
Replace the drive and restore from backup if the fault is confirmed.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did this start after a driver update, a new game/app install, or did it happen suddenly with no clear trigger?
clarifying_questions.purpose: A trigger event narrows the cause toward software (driver) vs hardware (GPU/cable).

clarifying_questions.prompt: Have you tried a different monitor or a different cable/port?
clarifying_questions.purpose: The fastest way to separate a monitor fault from a GPU/laptop fault.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Does your system have a separate graphics card, or does it use the built-in graphics on the CPU?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Determines whether reseating/testing a dedicated GPU is even applicable.

solution_steps.step: Check that the video cable is firmly connected at both the GPU/laptop and the monitor.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After reseating the cable, do you get a picture?

solution_steps.step: Try a different video cable or port (e.g. switch from HDMI to DisplayPort).
solution_steps.check_prompt: Does a different cable or port fix the display?

solution_steps.step: Confirm the monitor is set to the correct input source.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Was the input source set incorrectly?

solution_steps.step: Update or reinstall the graphics driver from the manufacturer's website (NVIDIA/AMD/Intel).
solution_steps.check_prompt: After updating the driver, is the display behaving normally?

solution_steps.step: Test the monitor with another device to rule out a monitor fault.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Does the monitor work correctly with another device?

solution_steps.step: If using a dedicated GPU, reseat it in its PCIe slot, or test with onboard graphics if available.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did reseating the GPU or switching to onboard graphics resolve it?

peripheral_issue.label: Peripheral Device Issue
causes: Faulty USB port or peripheral hardware

clarifying_questions.prompt: What's the exact device giving you trouble, and is it wired or wireless/Bluetooth?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Wireless issues need pairing-focused steps; wired issues need port/cable-focused steps.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did this device work before, or has it never worked on this computer?
clarifying_questions.purpose: A regression points to drivers/settings; never-worked points to compatibility or a dead unit.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Have you tried this device on another computer or in a different port?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Fastest way to isolate device fault vs port/system fault.

solution_steps.step: Unplug the device and plug it into a different port.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Does it work in a different port?

solution_steps.step: Test the device on another computer to confirm whether the device itself is faulty.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did it work on the other computer?

solution_steps.step: Open Device Manager and check for a yellow warning icon next to the device; update or reinstall its driver.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Was there a warning icon, and did updating the driver help?

solution_steps.step: For wireless devices, remove the existing Bluetooth pairing and re-pair the device.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did re-pairing fix the connection?

solution_steps.step: Restart the computer after reconnecting the device.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did the restart resolve it?

solution_steps.step: If the issue persists, the device or the port hardware may need replacement.

boot_recovery_issue.label: Boot / OS Recovery Issue
causes: Missing or corrupted bootloader / system partition
Wrong BIOS boot order or boot mode
Interrupted Windows update or recovery process
HP recovery environment / Sure Recover workflow was triggered

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did this start after a power loss, update, repair attempt, or drive replacement?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Helps distinguish corrupted boot files from a drive that is physically failing.

clarifying_questions.prompt: When you enter BIOS/UEFI setup, does the storage drive still appear in the boot or storage list?
clarifying_questions.purpose: If the drive is missing, the issue is more likely storage hardware than Windows itself.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Is there important data on the device that you need to preserve before recovery or reinstalling Windows?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Determines whether we should prioritize backup steps before any repair or reset.

solution_steps.step: If possible, back up important files first before any reset or reinstall.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Were you able to back up the important files?

solution_steps.step: Restart and enter BIOS/UEFI setup to confirm the storage drive is detected and the boot order is correct.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Is the drive detected in BIOS and set first in the boot order?

solution_steps.step: If the screen is an HP Sure Recover screen, press ESC to cancel if you only want to stop the recovery prompt, or follow the on-screen F11 recovery path if you intend to reinstall.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did ESC cancel the prompt, or did F11 take you to recovery options?

solution_steps.step: Use Windows Startup Repair or the HP recovery option for your model to repair the boot process.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did Startup Repair or recovery complete successfully?

solution_steps.step: If Windows still will not start, reinstall Windows using the recovery media or HP recovery workflow.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After reinstalling, does the computer boot normally?

solution_steps.step: If the drive is missing or fails diagnostics, replace the storage device before reinstalling Windows.

bios_firmware_issue.label: BIOS / UEFI Firmware Issue
causes: Incorrect boot order or boot mode
Firmware incompatibility after a hardware change
Secure Boot or legacy mode mismatch

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did the issue start after changing a BIOS setting, updating firmware, or installing new hardware?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Points directly to a BIOS/UEFI configuration problem.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Can you enter BIOS/UEFI setup successfully using the vendor key (often F10, F2, or Del)?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Shows whether the problem is before Windows even starts.

solution_steps.step: Enter BIOS/UEFI setup and load the default settings.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did loading BIOS defaults change anything?

solution_steps.step: Verify that the correct boot order, boot mode, Secure Boot, and storage mode are selected for your system.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Are the boot and security settings correct now?

solution_steps.step: If available, update the BIOS/UEFI firmware from the manufacturer's support page.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did the BIOS update finish successfully?

solution_steps.step: If the machine will not boot after a setting change, revert the last BIOS change you made.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did reverting the setting restore normal boot?

solution_steps.step: If you are not comfortable with firmware changes, ask a technician to apply the update safely.

driver_issue.label: Driver / Device Software Issue
causes: Windows Update replaced a working driver
Driver conflict after new hardware or software installation

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did the problem begin after a Windows update, driver update, or new hardware/software installation?
clarifying_questions.purpose: A recent update or install strongly suggests a driver/software cause.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Is the issue affecting one specific device or the entire system?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Helps separate a broken driver from a system-wide OS failure.

solution_steps.step: Open Device Manager and check for warning icons or unknown devices.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did Device Manager show a warning icon or unknown device?

solution_steps.step: Rollback the driver if the issue started immediately after an update.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did rolling back the driver help?

solution_steps.step: Uninstall the device and reinstall the latest driver from the manufacturer’s support page.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After reinstalling the driver, does the device work correctly?

solution_steps.step: Run Windows Update and install optional driver updates if offered.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did Windows Update offer a better driver?

solution_steps.step: If the problem persists, check the vendor support article for known compatibility issues.

application_issue.label: Application / Software Issue
causes: Incompatible app version or missing runtime
Conflict with another program or plugin

clarifying_questions.prompt: What app or program is failing, and does it fail at launch or only after you use it for a while?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Identifies whether the app itself or a workflow inside it is failing.

clarifying_questions.prompt: Did this start after an update, install, or plugin/change to the app?
clarifying_questions.purpose: Recent changes often point to a corrupted install or a compatibility issue.

solution_steps.step: Close the app completely and launch it again.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did restarting the app fix the issue?

solution_steps.step: Clear the app cache or reset the app settings if the program supports it.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did clearing the cache or resetting settings help?

solution_steps.step: Repair or reinstall the application from its official installer.
solution_steps.check_prompt: After repair or reinstall, does the app open normally?

solution_steps.step: Update the app and any required runtime packages such as .NET or Visual C++ redistributables.
solution_steps.check_prompt: Did updating the app and runtimes resolve it?

solution_steps.step: Check the app's official help page or release notes for known bugs.
